start.speaker: narrator
start.text: Brother Maren walked into the roots with his hands clasped and his eyes closed. He was praying — though to whom, in that moment, even he wasn't certain. The Ashen Light? The Heartwood? The villagers who watched in stunned silence? Perhaps all of them. Perhaps none.

the_taking.speaker: narrator
the_taking.text: The forest took him gently. The roots parted like arms opening for an embrace. His prayer continued — muffled, then distant, then gone. Where he stood, a small white flower bloomed. It smelled of incense and clean linen. The pact was renewed.

return_to_village.speaker: narrator
return_to_village.text: You return to Ashvale carrying Maren's prayer beads and nothing else. The crops are already recovering. The water clears. The blight recedes. But the Church of the Ashen Light has lost its shepherd, and the flock stands in the square with nowhere to turn.

faithful_react.speaker: narrator
faithful_react.text: Some of the faithful weep openly. Others stand rigid, faces carved from denial. A few simply leave — walk out the gate with their belongings and never look back, as though the village had become unholy ground. The Church's hold on Ashvale fractures in a single afternoon.

old_maren_speaks.speaker: old_maren
old_maren_speaks.text: I've lived long enough to see three priests come and go from this village. They all brought their gods with them, and they all left something behind when they went. Brother Maren left his courage. That's worth more than any sermon.

options.text: Maren's sacrifice was an act of faith. The Church should honor him as a saint.
The old faith couldn't save us. Maybe it's time for something new.
We don't need priests or gods. We need each other.

saint_path.speaker: narrator
saint_path.text: The Church declares Maren a saint — Saint Maren of the Roots, they call him. Pilgrims come from distant parishes to see the white flower that blooms where he fell. The Church endures, transformed by his example. It becomes gentler. Humbler. More willing to listen. Whether Maren would have approved is a question no one can answer. The dead keep their opinions to themselves.

new_faith_path.speaker: narrator
new_faith_path.text: A new faith emerges from the ashes of the old — one that honors the forest alongside the Light, that sees the divine not in doctrine but in the turning of seasons and the growth of living things. It has no name yet. It has no scriptures. But it has the white flower, and the memory of a man who gave everything. Sometimes that's enough to build a religion on.

secular_path.speaker: narrator
secular_path.text: The chapel empties. The prayer bells fall silent. Ashvale becomes a village of pragmatists — people who trust what they can see, touch, and grow. They honor Maren not with worship but with work: the fields he saved, the water he purified, the lives his death made possible. It's not faith. But it's something.

epilogue.speaker: narrator
epilogue.text: Years pass. The white flower at the edge of the Thornwood never wilts, never spreads, never dies. It simply blooms — a single point of impossible beauty in a world that often forgets such things exist. Villagers leave offerings there. Not prayers. Just... acknowledgments. A loaf of bread. A handful of grain. A child's drawing. The flower accepts them all with the same quiet grace. Some debts are paid in silence.
